examples.title: Noise Exposure Calculator
examples.description: Calculate time-weighted average noise exposure for occupational safety
examples.code:
// Calculate Time-Weighted Average (TWA) Noise Exposure
// Based on OSHA formula for 8-hour TWA

PROCEDURE CalculateTWA(exposures[], durations[])
 totalExposure <- 0
 totalDuration <- 0
 
 FOR i <- 0 TO length(exposures) - 1 DO
 // Convert dB to sound pressure squared ratio
 ratio <- 10 ^ (exposures[i] / 10)
 totalExposure <- totalExposure + (ratio * durations[i])
 totalDuration <- totalDuration + durations[i]
 END FOR
 
 // Calculate average exposure
 avgExposure <- totalExposure / totalDuration
 
 // Convert back to dB
 TWA <- 10 * log10(avgExposure)
 
 RETURN TWA
END PROCEDURE

// Check if exposure exceeds OSHA PEL (85 dB for 8 hours)
PROCEDURE CheckCompliance(TWA)
 IF TWA > 85 THEN
 RETURN "EXCEEDS LIMIT - Action Required"
 ELSE
 RETURN "Within Safe Limits"
 END IF
END PROCEDURE
examples.explanation: This algorithm calculates the time-weighted average noise exposure by converting decibel readings to their energy equivalents, averaging based on exposure duration, then converting back to dB. This is used in occupational safety to determine if workers need hearing protection.

examples.title: Sound Level Meter Reading Simulation
examples.description: Simulate decibel level measurement with logarithmic conversion from pressure
examples.code:
// Convert sound pressure (Pascals) to decibels
// Reference pressure: 20 μPa (threshold of human hearing)

CONSTANT REFERENCE_PRESSURE = 0.00002 // 20 μPa in Pascals
CONSTANT DB_THRESHOLD = 0

PROCEDURE PressureToDecibels(pressure)
 // Check for valid input
 IF pressure <= 0 THEN
 RETURN "Invalid pressure value"
 END IF
 
 // Calculate decibel level using formula:
 // dB = 20 * log10(pressure / reference)
 decibels <- 20 * log10(pressure / REFERENCE_PRESSURE)
 
 RETURN ROUND(decibels, 1)
END PROCEDURE

// Classify noise level for health assessment
PROCEDURE ClassifyNoiseLevel(dB)
 IF dB < 40 THEN
 RETURN "Quiet - No damage risk"
 ELSE IF dB < 70 THEN
 RETURN "Moderate - Possible annoyance"
 ELSE IF dB < 85 THEN
 RETURN "Loud - Risk with prolonged exposure"
 ELSE IF dB < 120 THEN
 RETURN "Very Loud - Hearing damage possible"
 ELSE
 RETURN "Dangerous - Immediate hearing damage risk"
 END IF
END PROCEDURE
examples.explanation: This pseudocode demonstrates the mathematical conversion of sound pressure to decibels using the standard formula. The 20 factor is used for pressure (as opposed to 10 for intensity), and demonstrates how the logarithmic scale represents the vast range of human hearing.
